comments.body:
Can one of the admins verify this patch?
I understand the following commands:
- `bot, add author to whitelist`
- `bot, test pull request`
- `bot, test pull request once`

Would like a test on this.  Just to make sure the environment variables get passed.  
Please make sure the environment variables do not end up in the container image.

Once you add command line options, you need to add them to man pages and command completions.

> Would like a test on this. Just to make sure the environment variables get passed.
> Please make sure the environment variables do not end up in the container image.
> 

These env vars are evaluated each time a 'buildah run' command is executed.  If you unset some of these vars between 'buildah run' commands, they cease to be present during the subsequent 'bulidah run command'

I've also confirmed that these do not persist 'buildah commit', both by inspecting the metadata and by `podman run --rm -it <image> env`

If you meant automated testing, I'm not sure where to start for that.

> Once you add command line options, you need to add them to man pages and command completions.

I might need a little help adding the command line options.  I'm sure I can figure out the man pages and bash completions.

Tests are failing, because your second PR was not signed.  It is better if you squash your commits together.

git rebase -i origin
This will load a screen which has the "f" or "fixup" option, which you can use to squash your PRs together.

As far as adding a flag, first thing you need to do is add a build options.

in buildah.go look for

CommonBuildOptions

Add you HttpProxy boolean option to this.

Now in your existing code wrap it with 
```
if b.CommonBuildOptions.HttpProxy {
}
```
 in cmd/buildah/from.go and cmd/buildah/from.go
Set the HttpProxy values in the section that sets up the BuilderOptions.

Now in pkg/cli/common.go look for FromAndBudResults, add a boolean there.  
Now look for how CPUQuota is handled and add similar calls for HttpProxy.

I've pushed my (failed) attempts to make the functionality optional.  The result complies, but the option is always disabled and the command line flags are not honored.  I'm likely missing something in connecting the setting of the option and reading of the option.

Side note: I also tested the original functionality of this PR in `buildah bud` and it worked exactly as expected.

I've gotten the '--http-proxy' option to work for 'buildah run' but not for 'buildah from' or 'buildah bud'.

Likely, I just haven't hooked things up properly.  Seems like there are several layers of indirection going on to get those hooked up.

I'll rebase everything in the end once I've gotten the functionality working; such rebase will ensure code formatting and signed-off-by headers are up to standards.
@jamescassell You would only see the --http-proxy working when doing a buildah run after you used it in the buildah from.

ready_for_review

Also, is there an easy way to add this to podman to have similar functionality when running containers there?

The tests don't like the naming of HttpProxy
```
# Run gofmt on version 1.11 and higher
buildah.go:286:2:warning: struct field HttpProxy should be HTTPProxy (golint)
Makefile:66: recipe for target 'validate' failed
make: *** [validate] Error 1
```
Rename it to make golint happy.

Also add --http-proxy options to contrib/completions/bash/buildah

And squash your PRs into one PR.

@jamescassell We will vendor this code into podman and that will handle the podman build case.  But we will need work to get this into 
podman run and podman create.
Closing since a squash was requested.  See #1525
